what_it_does: Describes the operational procedure for manually swapping the chat or embed default model on a single 16 GiB VRAM substrate when a model cannot co-reside with the always-on defaults.
observations: VRAM is the binding constraint: defaults use ~15.5 GiB, leaving no room for a fourth model.
Provides two CLI commands: `vllm-stack swap-chat <hf-id>` and `vllm-stack swap-embed <hf-id>`.
Lists swap-only candidates and models that fit co-resident with reasons.
Explains why a request-time model router is not implemented yet: VRAM, 2–3 minute cold start, and the substrate's always-on-defaults contract.
Documents state that survives a swap versus state that is lost.
Includes a restore-after-swap example using the default chat model.
Open topics include `swap-back`, `swap-list`, per-model flag profiles, Ollama parity, and a possible secondary endpoint.
Cross-references operations hub, VRAM budget, CLI, systemd services, and models index.
standouts: Explicit rejection of a request-time router because VRAM and cold-start UX make it worse than manual slot swapping.
Swap is framed as a first-class operational primitive rather than a hidden runtime routing detail.
The gap around per-model flag profiles is flagged as unresolved open work.
conventions: Uses frontmatter with type, title, date_started, and tags.
Uses wiki-link MOC pattern with `[[_operations-index|← Operations hub]]` and related links.
Commands shown in fenced code blocks; decision tables used for candidate classification.
Includes an 'Open at this topic' section for future work.
concerns: The `swap-chat` command uses a `sed` rewrite of `serve.sh`'s default-model line, which is brittle and assumes a specific quoting pattern.
Architecture-specific flags still require manual `serve.sh` edits, so the swap command is not fully self-contained for all listed candidates.
No mention of rollback or failure handling if a swap step stops a service but fails to start the replacement.
notes: Operational guide for manually swapping the chat/embed default model on a VRAM-constrained substrate, with rationale, exact commands, persistence rules, and open gaps.
questions: Where is `vllm-stack` implemented and what other subcommands does it expose?
What are the exact contents of `serve.sh` and `serve_embed.sh` beyond the default-model line?
How is the default model selected when multiple agents or callers use the endpoint concurrently?
What policy decides when to swap versus keeping a co-resident model loaded?
Are there monitoring or alerting hooks for OOM or swap failures?
intention_signals: Establish a clear, low-automation contract for switching models under a hard VRAM ceiling, making the cost, persistence semantics, and current substrate state explicit so callers understand when and why to swap.
novelty: Treats model swapping as a named, first-class operational primitive with dedicated CLI commands and documented persistence semantics, rather than hiding it behind a router or auto-scaling abstraction.
contribution: A file-local operational contract that records why manual slot swapping is used, the exact command surface, what state survives a swap, and the remaining gaps; a fused system would keep this as the canonical explanation of the swap primitive and its constraints.
summary_for_embedding: Operations document describing the `vllm-stack swap-chat` and `swap-embed` commands for replacing the default chat or embed model on a 16 GiB VRAM substrate, including swap candidates, persistence semantics, restore procedure, and rationale for not using a request-time router.
intention_for_embedding: To give operators and agents a clear, manual model-slot switching procedure and to document the VRAM and cold-start reasons that make automatic routing premature.
